mcqs.question: A process calls fork() which returns 0 to the child process. What does this indicate?

options.text: The fork failed due to insufficient memory
The child process was created successfully and this is the child process executing
The parent process has terminated
The child inherited all file descriptors from the parent

mcqs.explanation: fork() returns 0 in the child process upon successful creation. In the parent process, it returns the child's PID (positive value). A negative value indicates failure. The child does inherit copies of parent's file descriptors, but that's not what the return value indicates.

mcqs.question: Consider a process that executes: pid = fork(); if (pid == 0) { execve("/bin/ls", args, env); } else { wait(NULL); }. What is the sequence of operations?

options.text: Parent creates child, child exits immediately, parent waits and resumes
Parent creates child, child replaces itself with ls program, parent waits for child to complete
Parent and child both execute ls simultaneously
Parent executes ls, child waits for parent to finish

mcqs.explanation: fork() creates a child process. In the child (pid==0), execve() replaces the child's image with /bin/ls program. The parent calls wait(NULL) which blocks until the child (ls) completes. This is the standard pattern for process creation and program execution.

mcqs.question: Which system call is used to create a pipe for inter-process communication?

options.text: socket()
pipe()
msgget()
shmget()

mcqs.explanation: pipe() creates a unidirectional communication channel returning two file descriptors: one for reading, one for writing. socket() creates network sockets, msgget() creates message queues, and shmget() creates shared memory segments - all for IPC but with different characteristics.

mcqs.question: A process opens a file using open("data.txt", O_RDONLY) and receives file descriptor 3. If the process then calls close(3), what happens to the file descriptor 3?

options.text: File descriptor 3 is marked as available for reuse but the file remains open in the system
The file is closed and the descriptor is deallocated and available for immediate reuse
File descriptor 3 becomes invalid but the file remains open until all processes close it
An error occurs because you cannot close a descriptor directly

mcqs.explanation: close(3) closes the file descriptor 3 and releases it back to the system. The underlying file reference count is decremented; if it reaches zero, the file is closed. The descriptor is immediately available for reuse by subsequent open() calls.

mcqs.question: Which system call would be used to change the permissions of a file from read-write to read-only for owner?

options.text: chown()
chmod()
chgrp()
setuid()

mcqs.explanation: chmod() is used to change file permissions (mode). chown() changes file ownership, chgrp() changes group ownership, and setuid() sets the user ID of a process. To make a file read-only for owner, one would use chmod with appropriate permissions like 0o400.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the getpid() system call?

options.text: To get the parent process ID for creating new processes
To obtain the unique process identifier of the calling process
To retrieve the user ID of the current user
To get the process group ID for job control

mcqs.explanation: getpid() returns the process ID (PID) of the calling process. It's used for various purposes like creating unique identifiers, debugging, process management, and inter-process communication. getppid() returns parent PID, getuid() returns user ID, and getpgid() returns process group ID.

mcqs.question: A process calls read(fd, buffer, 1024) and receives a return value of 0. What does this indicate?

options.text: 1024 bytes were successfully read into the buffer
An error occurred during the read operation
End of file has been reached - no more data to read
The read operation is blocked and waiting for data

mcqs.explanation: A return value of 0 from read() indicates end-of-file (EOF) - the file position has reached the end of the file or the other end of a pipe/socket has closed. A positive value indicates bytes read, -1 indicates error, and blocking read would wait rather than return 0.

mcqs.question: Which system call allows a process to monitor multiple file descriptors simultaneously to check if any are ready for I/O?

options.text: poll()
select()
epoll()
Both B and C are correct

mcqs.explanation: Both select() and epoll() are I/O multiplexing system calls. select() is the traditional method with limitations (max fd count, O(n) performance). epoll() is a newer Linux-specific API offering better scalability. poll() is also an alternative. All enable monitoring multiple descriptors.

mcqs.question: What happens when a process calls exit(0) versus exit(1)?

options.text: exit(0) terminates immediately while exit(1) performs cleanup first
Both terminate the process identically; the argument is just an exit status code returned to parent
exit(0) closes all files while exit(1) leaves them open
exit(0) creates a core dump while exit(1) does not

mcqs.explanation: Both exit(0) and exit(1) perform identical process termination: flushing buffers, closing file descriptors, calling atexit handlers, and terminating the process. The argument (status) is merely an 8-bit exit code returned to the parent process via wait()/waitpid(). Conventionally, 0 indicates success, non-zero indicates failure.

mcqs.question: Which system call is NOT typically categorized under Process Control?

options.text: fork()
exec()
open()
wait()

mcqs.explanation: open() is a File Management system call, not Process Control. Process Control includes fork() (create process), exec() (execute program), wait() (wait for child), exit() (terminate), and related calls. File Management includes open, read, write, close, lseek, etc.

mcqs.question: A process uses the following sequence: int pipefd[2]; pipe(pipefd); write(pipefd[1], "hello", 5); read(pipefd[0], buf, 5); What is true about this code?

options.text: The pipe is unidirectional; data flows from index 1 to index 0 only
The pipe is bidirectional; data can flow in both directions
This code may deadlock because pipe buffers are limited
The file descriptors should both be even-numbered

mcqs.explanation: Pipes are unidirectional: pipefd[0] is the read end, pipefd[1] is the write end. Data written to pipefd[1] can only be read from pipefd[0]. Attempting to read from pipefd[1] or write to pipefd[0] would fail. This is a valid producer-consumer pattern with the pipe acting as a buffer.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the umask() system call?
mcqs.bits: umask() sets the file creation mask that determines default permissions for new files and directories

options.text: To set the current user's group ID for new processes
To set the file creation mask that determines default permissions for new files and directories
To unmask (enable) all file permissions that were previously blocked
To retrieve the current process's user ID

mcqs.explanation: umask() sets the file mode creation mask, which is bitwise ANDed with the complement of the requested permissions when creating new files/directories. For example, umask(022) means new files get 755 permissions (777 & ~022). It's a protection mechanism to restrict default permissions.

mcqs.question: Which system call would be most appropriate to obtain the current system time in seconds since epoch?

options.text: gettimeofday()
time()
clock_gettime()
Both B and C can provide this information

mcqs.explanation: Both time() and clock_gettime() with CLOCK_REALTIME can return seconds since epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). time() is the traditional simple interface. clock_gettime() provides higher resolution (nanoseconds) and is the modern replacement. gettimeofday() is deprecated in favor of clock_gettime().

mcqs.question: In the context of device management system calls, what is the primary function of ioctl()?

options.text: To read data from a device file similar to read()
To perform device-specific control operations that don't fit standard read/write semantics
To check if a device is ready for I/O
To list all available devices in the system

mcqs.explanation: ioctl() (Input/Output Control) is a device-specific system call for operations that don't fit standard read/write model. It's used for device configuration, queries, and control functions like setting terminal attributes, querying disk geometry, or configuring network interfaces. Each device defines its own ioctl commands.

mcqs.question: A process calls waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG). What does this accomplish?

options.text: Wait for any child to terminate without blocking, checking if any have already exited
Wait specifically for child with PID -1 which is invalid
Wait for all children sequentially
Block indefinitely until all children terminate

mcqs.explanation: waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) requests: wait for any child (-1 means any PID), store status in &status, and WNOHANG means return immediately if no child has terminated yet (non-blocking). This pattern is used in event loops to reap zombie processes without blocking.
